item.title: Who should Bangladesh be trading with?. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B, GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-OSS-20

The first answer is that we've got to stop thinking of it being a “country” that trades. The people within a country, yes. Organizations of companies inside it, yes. But the country itself doesn't trade. Maybe when the government buys aircraft or something. But nearly all trade is between people and organizations that just happen to be inside some lines on the map, not a country doing something. Therefore, because it's just people doing their own thing, it's not really for us to determine how much of it is done. It's all up to them -- the buyers and sellers. But we can still go further than this. Recent statistics show that China is the major trading partner of Bangladesh (21% of total trade) and then India and the US each about 8.5% -- India slightly ahead. The question we might want to ask ourselves is, well, is that about right? The standard theory of trade revolves around the “gravity model.” This works just like gravity and the planets -- bigger and closer has more effect than smaller and further away. So, the standard guide is that an economy will trade more with a larger economy and also more with one that is closer. So, a large economy that's close to us - China - should be a major trading partner. This seems obvious enough. The US is a long way away but it's also a very large economy so that seems fine. India is a very much smaller economy than either of those two yet it's right next door. That too seems fine. Which is about as far as most applications of this gravity model get. We should note that when people really calculate this, we find that the gravity model really does work. This really does explain global trade numbers. Except -- we do not mean mere geographical position when we talk about distance here. We mean, instead, economic distance. This is influenced by geography, yes, but isn't quite the same thing. What we really mean here is: “How difficult is it to do the trade over this distance?” So, transport links matter. In medieval Europe, for example, it was the sea and rivers that were the easy transport routes. So there could be -- and often was -- more trade between coastal Spain and coastal Italy than there was between the coast and 100 miles inland in either country. The roads were so bad, compared to water transport, that the economic distance over 500 miles on water could be shorter than that over 50 miles on land. We can also add in things like language as a barrier to trade, or whether the kings are at war. In this modern world, we also have to think about laws -- tariffs, customs posts, differences in standards that block trade. It's this last part that we can change. Which is the thing that really makes this concept of economic distance different from geographic distance. We can change economic distance by just changing our own barriers to trade. Do note that this is an area of economics where there's no right answer. Not one that we can point to and say: “This number should be this.” But once we translate the gravity model to the correct concept of economic - not just geographic - distance, Bangladesh probably doesn't trade enough with India. Yes, it's a smaller economy than China and the US, but it is also right next door. If that's true -- I would say it is even as others could argue that I'm wrong -- then one of the easy ways to make Bangladesh richer is simply to make it easier to trade with India. Sort out the land ports, make it easier and faster to clear goods through customs, lower the tariffs, and so on. But then I think Bangladesh should do that anyway, so it's not exactly a difficult conclusion for me to reach. Tim Worstall is senior fellow at Adam Smith Institute in London.

item.title: The FY2026-27 digital agenda. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B, GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-OSS-20

The national budget for FY 2026-27 signals a bold and aggressive policy shift aimed at completely transforming the digital ecosystem of Bangladesh. Officially elevating the information and communication technology sector into its top 10 strategic national priorities, the government has laid down a monumental challenge to expand the combined technology and telecommunications footprint from its current 1-2% to a staggering 10% of the nation's total gross domestic product within the next five years. To fund this ambitious leap into a high-tech future, the newly announced financial plan allocates Tk2,049 crore to the Information and Communication Technology Division and an additional Tk2,141 crore to the Posts and Telecommunications Division. These major resource allocations are tightly bound to wide-ranging structural tax and tariff adjustments designed to stimulate rapid market growth, though a comprehensive reading of the proposals reveals a complicated dual landscape of immense industry opportunities mixed with serious operational obstacles. For the domestic software arena and individual digital workers, this budget acts as an unprecedented financial springboard. The government has introduced a sharp reduction in advance tax from 5% down to 2%, coupled with a total withdrawal of import duties and value-added tax on fully built-up technology hardware components like laptops, desktop computers, enterprise servers, printers, flash memory, and high-resolution monitors. This dramatic regulatory rollback provides instant operational cost relief to established software companies expanding their physical tech architectures. At the same time, it significantly lowers the high economic entry barrier that previously restricted students, aspiring freelancers, and small boutique IT firms from accessing high-performance computational devices, thus instantly boosting the baseline capacity of the national technology workforce. The entrepreneurial ecosystem receives an equally vital lifeline through newly structured tax-free statuses and widespread value-added tax exemptions tailored for startups, digital content creators, and independent freelancers. This legislative support is further reinforced by a dedicated Tk500cr early-stage Startup Fund, which carries a strict state mandate to prioritize financing for youth and women-led digital commerce platforms and software collectives. Alongside these individual incentives, the macro digital economy is set to experience a surge in local enterprise demand via a massive structural push for digital public infrastructure (DPI), anchored by the launch of a unified national identity and digital wallet initiative. Building and managing these secure identity pipelines will create multi-year development projects for local system integrators and cybersecurity firms, while the seamless payment flow will bypass historical monetization bottlenecks for mobile application developers and e-commerce merchants alike. The telecommunications sector, which has historically operated under a punishing cumulative tax burden approaching 57%, finally receives some relief in this budget proposal. The withholding tax on standard mobile network services has been trimmed down from 12% to 10%. Far more significantly, the 20% withholding tax previously levied on revenue sharing, statutory license fees, and other ongoing regulatory charges payable to the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission has been completely dismantled. This systemic change liberates massive pools of working capital for telecom operators, allowing them to redirect resources toward expanding the essential 4-G and 5-G networks required to carry the intense data loads generated by the government's smart state initiatives. Furthermore, to protect the long-term goal of making Bangladesh a regional hub for technology exports, the administration has extended critical tax benefits for local mobile manufacturing plants until June 2030. This extension locks in lower advance income taxes of just 1% on 22 essential raw manufacturing materials, providing international investors with multi-year policy predictability. Despite this array of business-friendly incentives, the budget simultaneously introduces complex friction points and unintended negative consequences that could slow the country's digital trajectory if left unmanaged. While dropping import barriers on fully assembled laptops and servers is fantastic for technology users, it places domestic hardware assemblers at an immediate and devastating disadvantage. Local manufacturers operating within high-tech parks lose their protective competitive tariff cushions, leaving them vulnerable to cheaper, mass-produced global imports that can now flood the open market completely duty-free. A similar policy contradiction emerges in the consumer telecom sector. The outright abolition of the flat Tk300 SIM card tax is a highly progressive move intended to lower the initial connectivity barrier for low-income and rural subscribers. However, this relief is instantly counterbalanced by the introduction of a 15% VAT on the base supply price of all physical SIM cards and e-SIMs, meaning the onboarding cost for premium digital transitions will remain highly sticky. This shift also creates an immediate fiscal challenge, as removing the traditional SIM tax will drop state revenue by an estimated Tk1,200cr in the coming fiscal year. In an economy struggling to stabilize a massive project deficit, this steep decline will undoubtedly put pressure on the National Board of Revenue to enforce highly aggressive auditing measures or impose tighter compliance scrutiny across other flourishing segments of the local IT ecosystem. Finally, the budget reveals a disconnect between high-level digital policy goals and physical infrastructure realities. The state's aggressive vision for an AI-driven economy relies heavily on high-density GPU server rooms and advanced local cloud systems. Yet, these next-generation data centres require tremendous, uninterrupted electrical power. Because the budget proposal fails to include direct, heavy subsidies for green energy integration or guaranteed continuous heavy power grids for designated IT parks, local operators will face exorbitant running costs from backup generator systems, which threatens to completely wipe out the financial advantages gained from the duty-free import of enterprise servers. Similarly, the capital-intensive push toward microelectronics and semiconductor architecture is left exposed, as the budget lacks the direct cash-back incentives or specialized equity funds needed to attract physical wafer fabrication plants to the region. Ultimately, while the budget successfully transforms the tech sector from a simple tax target into an engine of growth, its ultimate success will depend entirely on how effectively the state addresses these underlying physical power, security, and local manufacturing challenges when implementation begins on July 1. Syed Almas Kabir is former President, BASIS and Chairman, Bangladesh ICT & Innovation Network (BIIN).

item.title: Closing Ad-din not the answer. [ thefinancialexpress ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Qwen-3-32B

The government's decision to cancel the licence of Ad-din Medical College Hospital imposed a harsh finality and most patients have already left the facility in search of alternative care. The Directorate General of Health Services chief Dr Pravath Chandra Biswas announced the revocation last Thursd
